Strip_Summary: The first thing that disappears is not a building. The internet. VPN spins and never catches.
No one asked what you thought about the war. It just arrived.
Glass of water on kitchen counter shakes. Vibration in your feet. Then the floor. Then the walls.
The sound comes last. It always comes last.
You start counting. Distance. Seconds. Bottles. People.
You don't remember when you started counting. But you don't stop.
Software engineer in Tehran. You build payment apps that can't process international transactions. Code works. Banking doesn't.
Boss sends thumbs-up emoji at two AM. That's your raise.
Wife teaches across town. Leaves before you wake. Puts daughter's shoes by front door every night. Lined up. Ready.
You don't know why that matters. It just does.
Tehran is nine million people on Hemmat Expressway. Students smoke on Enghelab Street. Coworker angry about Persepolis. That's the argument you're allowed.
Barista misspells your name on purpose. Saffron ice cream from the shop on Valiasr every Friday. Wife's favorite.
You carry ice cream home in plastic bag that always drips. Sticks to your fingers. Don't wipe them.
Daughter draws a cat. Orange body. Blue eyes. Doesn't look like a cat. She says it's a cat. So it's a cat.
She tapes it to the fridge. Every morning you see it. Press the tape back. It never holds.
The regime is background noise. Oppressive but familiar. Like weather. You've learned where the lines are. You live inside them.
Sanctions already here. Insulin costs three months salary. Pharmacy shelves have gaps. Dust on your fingertips.
Generic version from a factory that lost its supply chain. The pill exists somewhere. The logistics don't exist in yours.
You check the dollar exchange rate every morning before breakfast. Nothing you can do about the number. But you check.
You drop daughter at school. Backpack bigger than she is. She waves from the gate. You watch until she turns the corner. Every morning.
You thought these things would always be there. The metro. The cafe. The ice cream. The wave from the gate. You thought wrong.
It starts with the internet. Not with bombs.
Instagram dies Monday. WhatsApp Tuesday. VPN spins Wednesday and never connects. Phone screen becomes a mirror. Shows your face and nothing else.
When a government kills the internet it's not hiding information from the outside. It's building a wall around the inside.
Mother calls from neighbor's landline. Walked three blocks in slippers. Asks if you saw the news. There is no news. That is the news.
First airstrike hits twelve kilometers away. Vibration felt in your feet before sound. Glass of water on counter shakes. Then the sound.
Daughter wakes screaming. Wife grabs her. You grab the phone. No signal. Power cuts mid-grab.
In the dark your neighbor prays through the wall. Floor is cold. You didn't put on shoes. Don't remember standing up.
Something falls from the ceiling. Dust. Just dust. Wife whispers something you can't hear. Daughter's eyes wide open in the dark.
You count. One. Two. Three. If the next one is closer you move. This is the math now.
You don't pray. You count. Don't remember the last time you prayed. Or maybe you do. Just don't want to think about it.
You carry blankets and water and insulin and documents and one phone charger down the stairs. Plastic bag tears. Water bottle rolls. You leave it.
Daughter won't let go of the cat drawing. Carries it folded in her fist the entire night. Paper soft by morning.
Twelve families in the parking garage. Concrete floor. The smell. Engine oil and damp concrete and too many people. One battery radio.
A man you've never spoken to gives your daughter his jacket. You try to say thank you. He waves it off before you finish.
Someone brings bread they baked that morning. Nobody is in charge. Nobody needs to be.
Not military. Not government. Just people.
By the third night you stop asking if it's safe. Someone runs extension cord from generator two buildings over. Hum keeps children asleep. Keeps adults awake.
You try to explain it to someone once. Over the phone when it works for ten minutes. Words don't come out right. You stop trying.
After the strikes checkpoints go up on Hemmat. Men in uniforms patrol. Not to help. Not to evacuate. To watch.
A man taken because of a satellite dish. Comes back two days later. Says nothing about where he was. His wife doesn't ask. You don't either.
Your office shares a block with an IRGC logistics center behind a concrete wall with no sign.

You walked past it every morning for three years. Precision is a word that belongs to people who've never lived near the targets. From twelve kilometers away it's a strategy. From your kitchen it's the glass of water shaking.
You're being bombed from above and controlled from within. The war is above you. The regime is around you. You are in between.
Your jaw is tight. Not sure when that started. Water comes two hours a day. You fill every container.
Pots. Buckets. The bathtub. Daughter's plastic pool from last summer. You count the bottles.
Pharmacy. One remaining box of insulin. The man ahead says his son needs it. Your daughter is diabetic.
You both know there won't be another shipment. The pharmacist looks at both of you. Says nothing. The fluorescent light buzzes. You notice that.
You take the box. You say my daughter. Your voice doesn't sound like yours.
The man doesn't argue. He just stands there. You walk out. The box is lighter than expected. You look back once.
He is still standing at the counter.
That night your daughter sleeps. Cat drawing still in her fist. You sit in the dark. You don't sleep.
Your hands are shaking. You press them flat against your knees. They stop. Then they start again.
Apartment across the hall goes dark. Not a power cut. The family left. No goodbye. No forwarding address. Just a door that stays closed.
You walk past it every morning. You don't knock. You already know.
Borders close. Price of bus seat to Turkey triples overnight. Then triples again.
Colleague sold apartment for a third of its value. You calculate. Two seats. Not three.
You could sell the apartment. But who is buying in a city being bombed. You do the math again. Same answer.
Wife won't leave without you. You won't leave without mother. Mother says she's too old. Nobody leaves.
The math is always one person short.
Bread queue at five AM. By seven it wraps around the block. Faces lit by phone screens checking for signal.
Woman ahead has two children on her arms. Nobody helps her. You don't help her either.
Baker comes out. No more flour. Queue dissolves. Nobody riots. Nobody shouts. Everyone walks home with empty bags.
No one checks their phone anymore. There's nothing to check.
Eggs five times last month. Aspirin gone from every shelf. Man sells gasoline from plastic jugs. You count what's left.
Internet comes back for thirty minutes. Cousin in Canada calls. She's crying. She says the whole world is watching.
The world watches the explosions. Not the bread queue. Not the man at the pharmacy counter. Not twelve families sleeping on concrete.
Connection cuts. You only have time to say: we're alive. She says thank God. You don't correct her.
Daughter sitting on the floor. She looks up. Whose planes are those?
She's five. Thinks planes belong to someone the way toys do. She's right. Someone decided this in rooms you'll never enter.
You say: go draw the cat. She does. She draws the cat inside a box.
You don't ask what the box is.
You haven't taken a full breath in a while. You try. It catches halfway. You let it out. Close enough.
And then it stops. Just like that. When the ceasefire comes nothing returns. Not right away. Not ever.
Apartment has a crack ceiling to floor. You put your finger on it once. Plaster crumbles. You stop touching it.
Metro station nearest your office closed for structural damage. Different route now. Past buildings you don't recognize.
Company lost its servers. The rial fell further than you can calculate. You try anyway. Numbers don't help.
You go back to the office. Half the desks empty. Nobody cleared them out. Coffee cups still on some.
You open your laptop. The cursor blinks. You stare at it. Then start typing. Don't know what else to do.
Ice cream shop on Valiasr closed. Not bombed. Just closed. Owner left and didn't come back.
Cafe where barista misspelled your name still open. Different barista now. Gets your name right. Somehow that's worse.
You don't argue about football anymore. There's no one left to argue with.
Daughter flinches at car doors slamming. Every time.
She doesn't wave from the school gate anymore. Runs straight inside. You still watch until she turns the corner. Now you count how long it takes.
Wife still puts daughter's shoes by the door every night. Lined up. Ready. You don't ask ready for what.
She sleeps with the documents bag touching her arm. Every night.
Everything blurs. The crack. The drawing. Daughter's face. Then it comes back. You're standing in the kitchen.
You stop counting. Didn't decide to stop. It just happened. Like the war. Like everything.
You survived. Everyone says that's enough. Mother says God is merciful. Cousin says you're lucky.
Your wife doesn't say anything. She just checks the documents bag. It is not enough.
Cat drawing still on the fridge. The crack in the wall runs right through it. Orange body. Blue eyes. Masking tape still peeling.
Daughter sits at the table. She draws the cat again. This time inside a box.
You almost ask what the box is. You don't ask.
Only one number left. Alive.
